Backtest — DEU benchmark

DEU sample (test fixture)

3 resolved issues · search off (frozen benchmark) · sourced treaty-regime capabilities

Gate FAILED: the primary challenge model (MAE 16.67) must beat both baselines (capability x salience weighted mean 10.71; median actor position 16.67).

Challenge — rp-anchored, Q=0.9 (tuned split-sample) ★solver16.6719.1510.0030.00

Split-sample validation of the rp-anchored tuning

Tuned q = 0.9 on 2 train issues (candidates 0.3, 0.5, 0.7, 0.9), scored on 1 held-out issues. On the held-out half the tuned model (MAE 10.00) does NOT beat the equally-equipped weighted mean (MAE 10.00) — the improvement is not enough, and it is not an artifact of tuning.

Error distribution — primary model

Worst issues — for inspection

issueproposalforecastactualerror
t02i1sample beta50.080.030.0
t01i1sample alpha40.050.010.0
t01i2sample alpha50.040.010.0

Published DEU error rates — for context

The most-cited finding in this literature (Achen 2006, in Thomson et al.'s DEU project) is that the influence- and salience-weighted mean of member-state positions predicts EU outcomes as well as or better than the more complex bargaining and procedural models. Bueno de Mesquita's own tests on Thomson's data (2011) reproduce this: his 'Old Model' — the expected-utility / challenge model our solver reconstructs — records a mean absolute error around 21-28 on the 0-100 scale, losing to the simple weighted mean (~12-19). Our full-set result sits in the same regime and shows the same ordering.

published modelmean abs errorsubsetsource
Old Model (expected-utility / challenge)21.59 issues w/ resolve dataBdM 2011, Table 1
Weighted mean, round 111.89 issues w/ resolve dataBdM 2011, Table 1
Weighted median, round 129.49 issues w/ resolve dataBdM 2011, Table 1
Old Model (expected-utility / challenge)28.2issues w/o recursion pointBdM 2011, Table 3
Weighted mean, round 119.4issues w/o recursion pointBdM 2011, Table 3
Weighted median, round 119.8issues w/o recursion pointBdM 2011, Table 3
  • Bueno de Mesquita, B. (2011). A New Model for Predicting Policy Choices: Preliminary Tests. Conflict Management and Peace Science 28(1): 1-21. doi:10.1177/0738894210388127.
  • Achen, C. H. (2006). Institutional realism and bargaining models. In Thomson, Stokman, Achen & Konig (eds.), The European Union Decides. Cambridge University Press. (Finds the influence- and-salience-weighted mean of member positions did as well or better than more complex models.)
  • Arregui, J. & Perarnaud, C. (2021). A new dataset on legislative decision-making in the European Union: the DEU III dataset. Journal of European Public Policy. doi:10.34810/data53.
